items.title: Default / weak credentials
items.description: Application ships with default usernames and passwords that are rarely changed in production environments.

items.title: Brute-force / credential stuffing not rate-limited
items.description: Login endpoint allows unlimited attempts without lockout, CAPTCHA, or IP throttling.

items.title: Timing oracle in credential comparison
items.description: Password or token comparison uses non-constant-time comparison, leaking secret length or value through response timing.

items.title: JWT algorithm confusion (none / RS256→HS256)
items.description: Server accepts JWTs signed with 'none' algorithm or allows algorithm switching from asymmetric to symmetric, enabling token forgery.

items.title: Password reset token predictability
items.description: Password reset tokens are generated with weak RNG or are time-based and guessable, allowing account takeover.

items.title: OAuth / OIDC state parameter missing or reused
items.description: OAuth flow does not use or validate the state parameter, enabling CSRF attacks against the authorization callback.

items.title: Horizontal privilege escalation (IDOR)
items.description: Direct object references (user IDs, document IDs) are not validated against the authenticated user's ownership, allowing access to other users' resources.

items.title: Vertical privilege escalation
items.description: Lower-privileged users can access higher-privileged functionality by manipulating role parameters, path traversal, or parameter tampering.

items.title: Missing function-level access control
items.description: Administrative or sensitive API endpoints rely only on UI hiding rather than server-side authorization checks.

items.title: Mass assignment / parameter binding
items.description: Auto-binding of request parameters to model objects allows setting privileged fields (e.g., role, admin, price) not intended to be user-settable.

items.title: Session fixation
items.description: Session ID is not regenerated after successful login, allowing an attacker who set a known session ID to hijack the authenticated session.

items.title: Session token in URL
items.description: Session tokens are passed in URL query parameters, leaking them via Referer headers, browser history, and server logs.

items.title: Insecure session cookie flags
items.description: Session cookies missing Secure, HttpOnly, or SameSite=Strict attributes, enabling theft via XSS or CSRF.

items.title: Insufficient session expiry
items.description: Sessions remain valid indefinitely or for unreasonably long periods after logout or inactivity.

items.title: SQL injection
items.description: User input is concatenated directly into SQL queries without parameterization or sufficient escaping, enabling arbitrary query manipulation.

items.title: Command injection
items.description: User input is passed unsanitized to OS command execution functions (system, exec, popen), allowing arbitrary command execution.

items.title: Cross-site scripting (XSS)
items.description: User-controlled input is reflected in HTML responses without proper encoding, allowing injection of malicious scripts.

items.title: Path traversal
items.description: File path parameters accept '../' sequences allowing access to files outside the intended directory, potentially exposing sensitive system files.

items.title: XML / HTML entity injection (XXE)
items.description: XML parser processes external entity declarations from untrusted input, allowing SSRF, local file read, or DoS via billion-laughs.

items.title: ReDoS - catastrophic backtracking
items.description: Regular expressions applied to user input exhibit catastrophic backtracking behaviour, allowing DoS by crafting strings that trigger exponential evaluation time.

items.title: Integer overflow in parser
items.description: Attacker-controlled length/size fields from protocol or file parsers are used in arithmetic without overflow checks, yielding undersized allocations.

items.title: Hardcoded symmetric keys or secrets
items.description: Encryption keys, API tokens, or passwords are embedded in source code or compiled binaries, allowing extraction.

items.title: Weak or broken hash algorithm
items.description: MD5 or SHA-1 used for password hashing or integrity checks; susceptible to collision attacks and rainbow-table lookup.

items.title: Insecure random number generation
items.description: Cryptographic operations (key generation, token creation, IV selection) use non-cryptographic RNG (rand(), Math.random()), producing predictable output.

items.title: CBC padding oracle
items.description: Symmetric decryption error messages or timing differences reveal whether padding is valid, enabling plaintext recovery without the key.

items.title: Certificate validation disabled or incomplete
items.description: TLS certificate hostname or chain validation is bypassed in client code, enabling man-in-the-middle attacks.

items.title: Stack traces / verbose errors in production
items.description: Detailed error messages, stack traces, or internal paths are returned to clients, aiding attackers in reconnaissance.

items.title: Resource leak on error paths
items.description: Memory, file descriptors, or database connections are allocated before error conditions and not released in all exit paths, causing resource exhaustion or UAF.

items.title: Unchecked error return codes
items.description: Return values from security-sensitive functions (crypto operations, memory allocation, I/O) are ignored, allowing the program to continue in an invalid state.

items.title: Unrestricted file upload
items.description: File upload functionality does not validate file type or content, allowing upload of server-executable scripts (PHP, JSP, ASPX).

items.title: Path traversal in upload filename
items.description: The upload filename is used directly in a filesystem path without sanitization, allowing upload to arbitrary server directories.

items.title: Archive bomb (zip/tar bomb)
items.description: Uploaded archive files are extracted without checking decompressed size, enabling denial-of-service by exhausting disk or memory.

items.title: Broken object-level authorization (BOLA)
items.description: API endpoints expose object identifiers in request parameters without verifying that the caller has permission to access that specific object.

items.title: Excessive data exposure
items.description: API returns more data than the client UI displays; clients rely on presentation layer for filtering, leaking sensitive fields.

items.title: Lack of resource and rate limiting
items.description: API endpoints do not enforce request rate limits, allowing resource exhaustion attacks or mass enumeration.

items.title: GraphQL introspection enabled in production
items.description: GraphQL schema introspection is not disabled, allowing full enumeration of the API surface by unauthenticated clients.

items.title: HTTP request smuggling (CL.TE / TE.CL)
items.description: Inconsistent parsing of Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding headers between front-end proxy and back-end server allows request splitting and cache poisoning.

items.title: Server-side request forgery via URL parameter
items.description: The server fetches a URL supplied by the user without allowlist validation, enabling internal network scanning, cloud metadata access, or further pivoting.

items.title: DNS rebinding via SSRF
items.description: SSRF validation checks the hostname at request time but not at connection time, allowing DNS rebinding to bypass IP-based blocklists.

items.title: XML external entity injection
items.description: XML parser allows DOCTYPE declarations with SYSTEM entity references, enabling local file read, SSRF, or billion-laughs DoS.

items.title: XInclude injection
items.description: If DOCTYPE is disabled but xi:include processing is enabled, attackers can still trigger file inclusion attacks.

items.title: Suspicious maintainer activity (XZ-pattern)
items.description: Recent mass-commit from new contributor with unconventional build system changes - potential supply chain backdoor insertion.

items.title: Dependency confusion / typosquatting
items.description: Internal package names that could be registered on public registries, or package names close to popular ones, enabling malicious package substitution.

items.title: Build system executing downloaded code
items.description: Build scripts (CMakeLists, Makefile, configure) fetch and execute remote scripts during the build process without checksum verification.
